eventTypes.description: Fires once when a stock's live trade price crosses from at-or-above a threshold to strictly below it. Edge-triggered: if the price is already below the threshold when the subscription arms, it does not fire on that fact alone — it waits for an actual downward crossing. Set `resource` to the ticker symbol and `params.threshold` to the price level.

threshold.description: Price level in USD. Fires when the trade price crosses from at-or-above this value to below it.

metadata.durability: in-process watcher; lost on server restart

eventTypes.onWake: This fired because the price crossed below the threshold at some point in the past — it does not mean the price is still below it now, a turn later. Before acting on the user's original intent: call get_latest_price for the symbol and get_account for buying power, then re-check the threshold against that FRESH price. If it no longer holds (e.g. the price already bounced back above it), say so plainly and do not trade. Only proceed if the condition still holds.

eventTypes.description: Fires once when a stock's live trade price crosses from at-or-below a threshold to strictly above it. Edge-triggered, mirrors price.crossesBelow. Set `resource` to the ticker symbol and `params.threshold` to the price level.

threshold.description: Price level in USD. Fires when the trade price crosses from at-or-below this value to above it.

metadata.durability: in-process watcher; lost on server restart

eventTypes.onWake: This fired because the price crossed above the threshold at some point in the past — it does not mean the price is still above it now, a turn later. Before acting on the user's original intent: call get_latest_price for the symbol and get_account for buying power, then re-check the threshold against that FRESH price. If it no longer holds (e.g. the price already dropped back below it), say so plainly and do not trade. Only proceed if the condition still holds.

eventTypes.description: Fires once when a submitted paper order reaches a terminal state: filled, canceled, rejected, or expired — not filled alone, so the agent always learns the truth rather than waiting forever for a fill that will never come (the terminal status is included in the wake payload). Set `resource` to the Alpaca order id returned by submit_order. Delivered by Alpaca's trade_updates push stream; if the order is already terminal at the moment this subscription arms, it wakes immediately from a one-time REST check instead of waiting for a push event.

metadata.latency: websocket push (trade_updates), sub-second; one-time REST check at arm
metadata.durability: in-process watcher; lost on server restart

eventTypes.onWake: This subscription wakes on ANY terminal order status, not only a fill — check payload.snapshot.status before saying anything. Only "filled" is a success: report the symbol, filled quantity (payload.snapshot.filledQty), and average fill price (payload.snapshot.filledAvgPrice), and how that compares to the price that originally triggered the trade. Any other terminal status ("canceled", "rejected", "expired") is not a trade — say so plainly and never describe it as a successful purchase.

eventTypes.description: Fires when SEC EDGAR publishes a new filing for a company. Set `resource` to the ticker symbol (resolved to a CIK internally). Optionally set `params.formTypes` to filter to specific forms, e.g. ["8-K"].

formTypes.description: Optional filter, e.g. ["8-K", "10-Q"]. Omit to match any filing type.

metadata.durability: in-process poller; lost on server restart

eventTypes.onWake: Report the filing conversationally: form type (payload.snapshot.form), company (payload.snapshot.company), filing date (payload.snapshot.filingDate), and the accession number (payload.snapshot.accessionNumber); include the link (payload.snapshot.url) if useful. No re-check is needed — unlike a price level, a published SEC filing is immutable, so there's nothing to re-verify before reporting it.

eventTypes.description: Fires once at a specific future moment YOU choose — for waking yourself up on your own schedule instead of waiting on an external event or relying on a user/cron to intervene (e.g. "wake me when markets open Monday"). Set `params.at` to an ISO-8601 datetime string WITH AN EXPLICIT UTC OFFSET (Z is recommended; a bare offset-less datetime like "2026-07-14T09:30:00" is rejected, since there is no fixed server timezone to assume). Rejected at subscribe time if `at` is missing its offset, isn't a real calendar date/time, or isn't strictly in the future. Set `resource` to any short label for what you're waiting on (e.g. "market-open") — the clock provider itself doesn't use it operationally.

at.description: ISO-8601 datetime string WITH AN EXPLICIT OFFSET for when this should fire, e.g. "2026-07-14T09:30:00Z" or "2026-07-14T09:30:00-04:00". Z is recommended. Must be strictly in the future.

metadata.latency: ~0 in local dev (in-process timer); in production, this fires via a durable 30s sweep, so up to ~30s after the moment you set
metadata.durability: local dev: in-process timer, lost on server restart. Production (Vercel): a durable Redis-backed due-time index, survives restarts and deploys; a connector sweep checks it every ~30s

eventTypes.onWake: This is the timer YOU set — it is not an external event, so nothing about the world necessarily changed just because this fired. Time has passed since you subscribed: re-check whatever you were actually waiting on (prices, positions, filings, anything else relevant) before acting on it. Do not assume anything you believed true at subscribe time still holds now.
